description: A diagram showing the Transformers library as a central 'pivot' or hub connecting model definitions to downstream frameworks. It likely depicts Transformers at the center with spokes or arrows pointing outward to training frameworks (Axolotl, Unsloth, DeepSpeed, FSDP, PyTorch-Lightning), inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TGI), and adjacent modeling libraries (llama.cpp, mlx).
teaching_value: Visually communicates the architectural role of the Transformers library as a single source of truth for model definitions, helping students understand why ecosystem compatibility matters and how one library can serve as an interoperability layer across many tools.
when_to_show: Show this when introducing the Transformers library for the first time, especially when explaining why a student should learn Transformers rather than a framework-specific API. Also useful when discussing MLOps tooling choices.
suggested_caption: Transformers as the central model-definition hub across ML frameworks. (HuggingFace)

description: A text-to-image example showing a text prompt input on the left and the resulting AI-generated image output on the right, illustrating the basic input/output interface of Stable Diffusion
teaching_value: Grounds students in the concrete task before diving into internals — establishes what the system does at the user-facing level
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on Stable Diffusion to anchor the discussion in a familiar, tangible example before introducing architecture
suggested_caption: Stable Diffusion: text prompt in, generated image out. (Alammar)

description: A diagram illustrating image-to-image (img2img) generation in Stable Diffusion, showing both an input image and a text prompt feeding into the system to produce a modified output image
teaching_value: Extends student understanding beyond text-only generation to show that Stable Diffusion supports multiple input modalities, helping learners appreciate the system's versatility
when_to_show: Show after introducing text-to-image to contrast the two modes and prompt discussion about how image information is incorporated alongside text
suggested_caption: img2img: Stable Diffusion accepts both image and text inputs. (Alammar)

description: A high-level system diagram showing two main blocks: a 'Text Understanding' component (text encoder) on the left receiving a text prompt, and an 'Image Generator' component on the right producing the output image, with an arrow showing information flow between them
teaching_value: Introduces the modular architecture of Stable Diffusion at a high level, helping students see that the system is composed of distinct, separable components rather than a single black box
when_to_show: Show when first explaining that Stable Diffusion is not monolithic — use as a scaffold before drilling into each component individually
suggested_caption: Stable Diffusion: text encoder feeds numeric representations to image generator. (Alammar)

description: A diagram expanding the image generator block into two sub-stages: (1) Image Information Creator and (2) Image Decoder, with text embeddings flowing in from the text encoder on the left
teaching_value: Reveals the two-stage structure inside the image generator, helping students understand that image creation is a sequential process — first building latent information, then decoding to pixels
when_to_show: Show immediately after the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into the image generator and introduce the concept of latent space processing
suggested_caption: Image generator internals: information creator then decoder. (Alammar)

description: A diagram highlighting the Image Information Creator component, showing it operates iteratively over multiple steps (the 'steps' parameter) in latent space, with a UNet and scheduler inside, before passing output to the image decoder
teaching_value: Explains the role of the 'steps' parameter students encounter in Stable Diffusion interfaces, connecting a practical UI control to the underlying iterative diffusion process in latent space
when_to_show: Show when explaining why increasing steps improves quality, or when students ask what the 'steps' parameter does in generation interfaces
suggested_caption: Image information creator: UNet + scheduler iterate in latent space. (Alammar)

description: A complete architecture diagram labeling all three neural network components of Stable Diffusion: CLIPText (text encoder), UNet (diffusion in latent space), and Autoencoder Decoder (pixel image painter), with their connections shown
teaching_value: Provides a consolidated reference view of the full system with precise component names, enabling students to map informal descriptions to technical model names they will encounter in papers and code
when_to_show: Show as a summary diagram after introducing each component individually, or as a reference when students need to connect component names to their roles
suggested_caption: Three neural networks in Stable Diffusion: CLIPText, UNet, Autoencoder. (Alammar)

description: A detailed tensor-level architecture diagram showing the exact data shapes flowing between components: text embeddings (77×768), noise latent tensor (4×64×64) into UNet+Scheduler, processed latent (4×64×64) into Autoencoder Decoder, and final image (3×512×512)
teaching_value: Makes abstract data flow concrete by showing exact tensor dimensions at each stage, helping students understand how information is represented numerically and why latent space is more efficient than pixel space
when_to_show: Show when students are ready for technical depth — ideal when discussing why latent diffusion is faster than pixel-space diffusion, or when connecting to code implementations
suggested_caption: Tensor shapes through Stable Diffusion: 77×768 → 4×64×64 → 3×512×512. (Alammar)

description: A step-by-step diffusion process diagram showing how a random noise latent array is progressively refined over multiple steps using text embeddings and the UNet, with each step producing a less noisy latent until the final array is decoded into an image
teaching_value: Visualizes the core diffusion loop, making the iterative denoising process intuitive and showing how text conditioning guides each step toward a coherent image
when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of diffusion — use to explain why the process is called 'diffusion' and how text guides the denoising trajectory
suggested_caption: Diffusion: random noise latent iteratively refined using text embeddings. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing multiple UNet processing steps in sequence, with each step taking the current latent and text embeddings as input and outputting a refined latent, illustrating the repeated application of the UNet across diffusion steps
teaching_value: Clarifies that the same UNet is applied repeatedly (not different networks per step), helping students understand the role of the scheduler in controlling how much denoising occurs at each step
when_to_show: Show when explaining the UNet's role in the diffusion loop, especially to clarify the relationship between the number of steps and the UNet's repeated application
suggested_caption: UNet applied repeatedly: each step refines the latent toward the prompt. (Alammar)

description: A visual sequence of decoded latent arrays at multiple diffusion steps (e.g., steps 1, 2, 4, 10, 20, 50), showing the progression from pure visual noise to a recognizable, detailed image, demonstrating how structure emerges during denoising
teaching_value: Provides compelling visual evidence of the denoising process, making abstract math tangible — students can see how an image 'emerges from noise' and understand why more steps yield better results up to a point
when_to_show: Show as the key intuition-building moment when teaching diffusion — particularly effective when students ask 'what is actually happening at each step?' or when discussing the steps parameter
suggested_caption: Denoising steps visualized: structure emerges from noise step by step. (Alammar)

description: A sequence of 5 images showing a photograph progressively transformed from clean (step 0) to pure noise (step 4), illustrating the forward diffusion process used to generate training examples
teaching_value: Concretely shows students how noise is incrementally added to a real image, making the abstract concept of 'forward diffusion' tangible and demonstrating how each noisy version becomes a labeled training example
when_to_show: Introduce this when first explaining how diffusion models are trained — show it before discussing the noise predictor to establish what the model is learning to reverse
suggested_caption: Forward diffusion: clean image (step 0) to pure noise (step 4). — Alammar

description: A diagram showing a single forward diffusion training example: an original image plus a generated noise sample combined to produce a noisy image, with the noise amount labeled, forming one (noisy_image, noise, step) training tuple
teaching_value: Clarifies the exact structure of one training example — input image, added noise, and noise level — helping students understand what supervised signal the noise predictor is trained on
when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of forward diffusion and before explaining the noise predictor architecture, to bridge 'how noise is added' with 'what the model learns'
suggested_caption: One training example: original image + noise = noisy image at step t. — Alammar

description: A diagram showing multiple training examples fed into a U-Net noise predictor, illustrating how a dataset of (noisy image, noise level) pairs is used to train the model to predict the noise component
teaching_value: Shows students the scale and structure of the training dataset — many (noisy image, step) pairs — and how they collectively train the U-Net, connecting data generation to model training
when_to_show: Use after explaining individual training examples to show how they aggregate into a full training regime; ideal before discussing the training loss or optimization step
suggested_caption: Many noisy training examples train the U-Net noise predictor. — Alammar

description: A diagram of a single U-Net training step: a noisy image and timestep are input, the U-Net predicts noise, the predicted noise is compared to the actual noise via a loss function, and gradients update the model weights
teaching_value: Illustrates the complete supervised training loop for the noise predictor — input, prediction, loss computation, and weight update — giving students a concrete ML training workflow to anchor the abstract diffusion concept
when_to_show: Present when transitioning from 'what training data looks like' to 'how the model is optimized'; useful for students with ML background to connect diffusion to familiar training paradigms
suggested_caption: U-Net training step: predict noise, compute loss, update weights. — Alammar

description: A diagram of one denoising step during inference: a noisy image and timestep are fed into the trained noise predictor, which outputs a predicted noise slice that is subtracted from the noisy image to produce a slightly cleaner image
teaching_value: Shows students the reverse diffusion step in isolation, making clear that generation is iterative subtraction of predicted noise — the inverse of the training process — and grounding the abstract 'reverse process' concretely
when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the 'generating images' section, immediately after training is explained, to show how the trained model is used in the opposite direction
suggested_caption: One denoising step: subtract predicted noise to recover a cleaner image. — Alammar

description: A diagram showing a second denoising step chained after the first, illustrating that the output of one denoising step becomes the input of the next, progressively reducing noise across multiple iterations
teaching_value: Demonstrates the iterative, chained nature of the reverse diffusion process — students see that generation requires many sequential steps, not a single pass, reinforcing why sampling is computationally expensive
when_to_show: Show immediately after the single denoising step diagram to extend the concept to a full generation loop; useful for motivating later discussion of faster samplers like DDIM
suggested_caption: Chained denoising steps progressively reconstruct an image from noise. — Alammar

description: A full end-to-end image generation diagram showing the complete reverse diffusion pipeline: starting from pure random noise, applying many sequential denoising steps via the noise predictor, and arriving at a final generated image
teaching_value: Gives students the complete picture of image generation by diffusion — from random noise input to coherent image output — synthesizing all prior concepts into one unified visual workflow
when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining individual denoising steps; ideal for summarizing the generation process before introducing text conditioning or latent diffusion
suggested_caption: Full image generation: iterative denoising from pure noise to final image. — Alammar

description: A diagram of an autoencoder showing an input pixel image compressed by an encoder into a smaller latent representation, then reconstructed back to pixel space by a decoder, with the latent space highlighted as the site of diffusion
teaching_value: Explains why Stable Diffusion is computationally efficient — diffusion operates in compressed latent space rather than full pixel space — and introduces the encoder/decoder components students need to understand the full pipeline
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from basic diffusion to Stable Diffusion's specific architecture; use to motivate why latent diffusion is faster before showing the full latent diffusion pipeline
suggested_caption: Autoencoder compresses images to latent space where diffusion operates. — Alammar

description: A diagram showing the forward diffusion process applied to latent representations rather than pixel images: an image is encoded to a latent, noise is progressively added to the latent across timesteps, generating latent-space training examples
teaching_value: Shows students the concrete adaptation of forward diffusion to latent space — noise is added to compressed latents, not pixels — clarifying what the noise predictor actually operates on in Stable Diffusion
when_to_show: Show after the autoencoder diagram to demonstrate how forward diffusion is adapted to latent space; bridges the autoencoder concept with the diffusion training process
suggested_caption: Forward diffusion applied to compressed latents, not raw pixels. — Alammar

description: A two-path diagram (mirroring Figure 3 from the LDM paper) showing the forward process (image → encoder → noisy latents) and reverse process (noisy latents → denoising U-Net → decoder → generated image), with conditioning inputs (text prompts) feeding into the U-Net
teaching_value: Unifies the entire Stable Diffusion architecture in one diagram — encoding, latent diffusion, conditioning, and decoding — allowing students to see how all components interact and where text control enters the pipeline
when_to_show: Use as the master architecture diagram after all individual components have been introduced; ideal for synthesis discussions or as a reference when students ask how text prompts influence generation
suggested_caption: Complete LDM architecture: forward/reverse latent diffusion with text conditioning. — Alammar

description: Full architecture diagram of Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion) showing the forward diffusion process on compressed latents via an autoencoder encoder, the noise predictor (UNet) operating in latent space, the reverse process via the autoencoder decoder, and the text conditioning pipeline feeding into the noise predictor.
teaching_value: Gives students a single unified view of how all major components — autoencoder, latent space, noise predictor, and text conditioning — connect, making abstract relationships concrete and traceable.
when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing the full Stable Diffusion pipeline, after students understand basic diffusion, to show how pixel-space diffusion is replaced by latent-space diffusion with conditioning.
suggested_caption: Stable Diffusion architecture: latent diffusion with text conditioning. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing a Transformer language model (ClipText, a GPT-based model) taking a text prompt as input and producing token embeddings, which are then fed into the image generation process as conditioning vectors.
teaching_value: Clarifies the specific role of the text encoder in Stable Diffusion — it is not generating images but producing embeddings that guide the noise predictor, helping students distinguish the language and image components.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how text prompts are converted into a form the UNet can use; ideal after introducing the overall architecture and before explaining cross-attention.
suggested_caption: ClipText encodes text prompts into token embeddings for image conditioning. (Alammar)

description: Illustration of a sample image-caption dataset used to train CLIP, showing paired examples of images and their descriptive text captions, representing the 400-million-pair web-crawled training corpus.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract concept of contrastive training data in a concrete visual example, helping students understand what the training signal for CLIP looks like before learning the training procedure.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the CLIP training explanation, before introducing the contrastive loss, to establish what kind of data CLIP learns from.
suggested_caption: CLIP trains on 400M image-caption pairs crawled from the web. (Alammar)

description: Step 1 of CLIP training: an image and its caption are each passed through their respective encoders (image encoder and text encoder) to produce embeddings, which are then compared using cosine similarity — shown to be low at the start of training.
teaching_value: Illustrates the first step of contrastive learning — encoding a matched pair and measuring initial low similarity — making the training objective tangible before showing how the models are updated.
when_to_show: Show as the first step in a three-step walkthrough of CLIP training, immediately after presenting the dataset diagram.
suggested_caption: CLIP step 1: encode image and caption, compare embeddings via cosine similarity. (Alammar)

description: Step 2 of CLIP training: the image and text encoders are updated via backpropagation so that the embeddings of a matched image-caption pair become more similar, illustrating the gradient update step.
teaching_value: Shows the optimization step of contrastive learning — that the model parameters are adjusted to bring matching pairs closer in embedding space — completing the learning loop for students.
when_to_show: Show as step 2 in the CLIP training walkthrough, directly after the initial encoding and similarity measurement step.
suggested_caption: CLIP step 2: update encoders so matching image-caption embeddings become similar. (Alammar)

description: Step 3 of CLIP training: after repeated training across the dataset with large batch sizes, the encoders produce aligned embeddings where semantically matching image-text pairs have high cosine similarity, and negative (non-matching) pairs have low similarity.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the end result of CLIP training — a shared embedding space where images and text are aligned — and introduces the critical concept of negative examples needed for contrastive learning to work.
when_to_show: Show as the final step of the CLIP training walkthrough to summarize what the trained model achieves and why negative examples are necessary.
suggested_caption: CLIP step 3: trained encoders align matching image-text pairs in embedding space. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the inputs to the Stable Diffusion UNet noise predictor: a noisy latent image, a timestep value, and encoded text token embeddings — all operating in latent space rather than pixel space.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students all three inputs the UNet receives simultaneously, clarifying that text conditioning is an additional input alongside the noisy latent and timestep, not a replacement.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from explaining CLIP embeddings to explaining how those embeddings are used inside the UNet; bridges the text encoder and the denoising network.
suggested_caption: UNet inputs: noisy latent, timestep, and text token embeddings in latent space. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of the training dataset for the text-conditioned noise predictor, showing triplets of (noisy latent image, encoded text embedding, target noise) used to train the UNet to predict noise conditioned on text.
teaching_value: Makes explicit what the training data looks like when text conditioning is added, helping students understand how the model learns to associate text embeddings with specific noise patterns.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the noise predictor is trained with text conditioning, to ground the abstract conditioning concept in a concrete data format.
suggested_caption: Training dataset: noisy latents paired with text embeddings and target noise. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a diffusion UNet without text conditioning, showing its inputs (noisy latent image and timestep) and output (predicted noise), with internal ResNet blocks visible — serving as a baseline before text conditioning is added.
teaching_value: Establishes a clear baseline architecture so students can precisely identify what changes when text conditioning is introduced, making the architectural modification more meaningful.
when_to_show: Show before introducing the text-conditioned UNet to establish the baseline; use as a 'before' in a before/after comparison when explaining cross-attention addition.
suggested_caption: Baseline UNet noise predictor: noisy latent and timestep inputs, no text. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the internal layers of the UNet noise predictor with ResNet blocks, illustrating the sequential processing steps within the encoder and decoder paths of the UNet before attention layers are added.
teaching_value: Reveals the internal structure of the UNet's ResNet blocks, helping students understand the building blocks that will be augmented with cross-attention layers for text conditioning.
when_to_show: Show when diving into UNet internals, after the high-level UNet diagram, to prepare students for understanding where attention layers are inserted for text conditioning.
suggested_caption: UNet internal ResNet block layers before text-conditioning attention is added. (Alammar)

description: Architecture diagram of a UNet noise predictor modified for text conditioning, showing ResNet blocks interleaved with cross-attention layers that receive text embeddings as input, with labeled inputs (noisy latent + timestep + text tokens) and outputs (predicted noise).
teaching_value: Visually contrasts the text-conditioned UNet against the text-free version, making explicit where and how text representations are injected via attention layers between ResNet blocks — clarifying that ResNet blocks process spatial features while attention layers merge text context into the latents.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the text-free UNet, when explaining how text prompts influence image generation — use it to highlight the structural addition of cross-attention layers and why ResNet blocks alone cannot process text.
suggested_caption: Text-conditioned UNet: cross-attention layers inject text embeddings between ResNet blocks. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step walkthrough diagram of the text-conditioned UNet noise predictor, illustrating the sequential flow of data through alternating ResNet and cross-attention layers, showing how text token representations are repeatedly merged into the evolving latent representation at each stage.
teaching_value: Provides a dynamic, step-by-step view of information flow through the conditioned UNet, reinforcing that text conditioning is applied iteratively at multiple depths of the network — helping students understand why deeper layers can leverage progressively richer text-fused representations.
when_to_show: Show after the static architecture diagram (Image 1), when students need to understand the sequential processing order — particularly useful when discussing why text influence accumulates across network depth.
suggested_caption: Step-by-step data flow through text-conditioned UNet layers. (Alammar)

description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text tokens and visual region embeddings (each summing tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how visual features are treated as pseudo-tokens alongside text tokens, making the fusion mechanism tangible and comparable to standard BERT token embeddings.
when_to_show: When introducing the 'joint text-image training' paradigm; use to contrast how image regions are embedded analogously to word tokens in BERT.
suggested_caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)

description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, pre-training stages).
teaching_value: Demonstrates empirically which components of VisualBERT's training (e.g., visually-grounded objectives, pre-training on COCO) contribute most to performance, teaching students how to interpret ablation studies.
when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss how researchers isolate the contribution of each design choice.
suggested_caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)

description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a bidirectional prefix encoder (cross-attention) and a text decoder with causal (unidirectional) attention, illustrating the prefix language model design.
teaching_value: Visually distinguishes the bidirectional prefix attention over image tokens from the causal autoregressive text decoder, clarifying the hybrid attention mechanism central to SimVLM.
when_to_show: When introducing SimVLM; use to contrast bidirectional vs. causal attention and explain why images are encoded as prefix tokens.
suggested_caption: SimVLM encodes image patches as bidirectional prefix; text is decoded autoregressively. (Wang et al. 2022)

description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants such as different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM) and data mixtures (image-text only vs. combined with text-only C4 data).
teaching_value: Illustrates the importance of the PrefixLM objective and mixed training data, helping students understand how pre-training design choices affect downstream VQA performance.
when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to reinforce why both the objective choice and data diversity matter in vision-language pre-training.
suggested_caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating the causally-masked multimodal modeling (CM3) approach, showing how HTML-structured web content with interleaved text and image tokens is processed with a causal masking strategy that allows both generation and infilling.
teaching_value: Shows how CM3 unifies causal and masked language modeling over multimodal hypertext, helping students understand how web-scale structured data enables flexible conditional generation.
when_to_show: When contrasting CM3 with simpler joint-training models; use to explain how masking strategy enables both generation and infilling in a multimodal context.
suggested_caption: CM3 applies causal masking to interleaved text and image tokens from HTML pages. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)

description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training where only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) is updated to produce image embeddings compatible with a frozen GPT-style LM; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image embeddings prepended to text.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the frozen LM paradigm — only the vision encoder is trained — and shows how image embeddings act as a visual prefix, enabling few-shot generalization without modifying the LM.
when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' approach; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain the motivation for freezing LM parameters.
suggested_caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM remains fixed, enabling few-shot VQA. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

description: Two bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants (Frozen, Frozen-scratch, Frozen-finetuned, Frozen-train-blind) on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right), trained on Conceptual Captions.
teaching_value: Empirically demonstrates that freezing the LM outperforms fine-tuning it and training from scratch, and that even a blind baseline scores non-trivially — teaching students about the value of pre-trained LM knowledge.
when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion on why fine-tuning the LM hurts performance and what the blind baseline reveals about LM priors.
suggested_caption: Frozen LM outperforms fine-tuned variant on VQA; blind baseline shows LM prior strength. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings, a lightweight mapping network (transformer or MLP) translating them into k prefix vectors in GPT-2 embedding space, and a frozen GPT-2 generating captions.
teaching_value: Highlights the role of the mapping network as a bridge between CLIP's visual embedding space and GPT-2's linguistic space, illustrating how minimal trainable parameters can enable vision-language generation.
when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to show how a mapping network replaces direct vision encoder training and discuss the trade-off between transformer vs. MLP mapping.
suggested_caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix vectors via a lightweight network. (Mokady et al. 2021)

description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', etc.).
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, interpretable view of what the mapping network learns — that visual prefixes encode semantically meaningful linguistic concepts — reinforcing the idea of cross-modal embedding alignment.
when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's mapping network; use as an intuition-building example to show that learned visual prefixes are not arbitrary but carry semantic meaning.
suggested_caption: ClipCap's image prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words. (Mokady et al. 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of VisualGPT showing a visual encoder feeding into a GPT-style decoder via cross-attention, with the self-resurrecting activation unit (SRAU) gating mechanism that balances visual information and pre-trained linguistic knowledge using complementary gates B_vis and B_lan.
teaching_value: Illustrates how a gated cross-attention mechanism can dynamically balance visual and linguistic signals at each decoder layer, teaching students about fine-grained fusion strategies beyond simple concatenation.
when_to_show: When introducing cross-attention fusion mechanisms; use to contrast with prefix-based approaches and explain how complementary gates prevent catastrophic forgetting of linguistic knowledge.
suggested_caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals. (Chen et al. 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning metrics across data-scarce conditions
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical advantage of adapting pre-trained LMs with minimal in-domain data, helping students understand the data efficiency gains from the self-resurrecting mechanism
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SRAU gating mechanism to illustrate why balancing linguistic and visual signals matters empirically, especially in low-data regimes
suggested_caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a language decoder (GPT-2), and a self-ensemble module combining vision-language fused logits with language-only logits
teaching_value: Visually clarifies how VC-GPT avoids catastrophic forgetting by inserting cross-attention layers between frozen pretrained components rather than modifying them directly, and shows the self-ensemble fusion step
when_to_show: Show when introducing VC-GPT to illustrate the architectural solution to catastrophic forgetting before presenting the self-ensemble logit combination formula
suggested_caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT-2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)

description: Results table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics
teaching_value: Quantifies the contribution of the self-ensemble module and contextualizes VC-GPT's performance relative to other models, teaching students to read ablation tables and interpret captioning metrics
when_to_show: Show after explaining the self-ensemble formula to demonstrate its empirical importance via the 'VC-GPT w/o SE' ablation row
suggested_caption: Self-ensemble module significantly boosts VC-GPT captioning performance. (Luo et al. 2022)

description: Two-panel diagram: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching training in MERLOT; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and a temporal frame reordering task on shuffled video segments
teaching_value: Illustrates how MERLOT learns both spatial and temporal multimodal representations simultaneously through two complementary objectives, helping students understand multi-task video-language pretraining
when_to_show: Show when introducing MERLOT to contrast image-only vs. video-based pretraining and to explain why temporal reordering is a useful self-supervised objective
suggested_caption: MERLOT learns spatial and temporal video-language alignment via two objectives. (Zellers et al. 2021)

description: High-level overview diagram of Flamingo showing interleaved image/text inputs processed by a frozen vision encoder and Perceiver resampler, with cross-attention layers inserted between frozen LM layers to inject visual features
teaching_value: Shows the full Flamingo pipeline and the key design choice of interleaving cross-attention layers with frozen LM layers, helping students understand how visual grounding is injected without retraining the LM
when_to_show: Show at the start of the Flamingo section to establish the overall architecture before diving into the gated cross-attention details
suggested_caption: Flamingo injects visual features into a frozen LM via Perceiver and cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Detailed architecture diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how a tanh-gated cross-attention output is added to the frozen LM's feed-forward output with a learnable scalar gate initialized to zero
teaching_value: Explains the gating mechanism that allows stable training by initializing visual influence at zero and gradually learning to incorporate it, teaching students about training stability techniques in multimodal models
when_to_show: Show after the Flamingo overview to explain the specific mechanism preventing destabilization of the pretrained LM during multimodal training
suggested_caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer enables stable visual integration. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Line plots showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot to 32-shot) across multiple vision-language benchmarks
teaching_value: Demonstrates emergent few-shot learning in large vision-language models and the scaling laws for both model size and in-context examples, key concepts for understanding modern large multimodal models
when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's few-shot capabilities to illustrate that both model scale and number of examples matter, and to motivate why large-scale pretraining is valuable
suggested_caption: Flamingo performance scales with both model size and few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo variants (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning
teaching_value: Provides a holistic view of Flamingo's capabilities relative to task-specific fine-tuned models, teaching students to evaluate trade-offs between few-shot generalist models and fine-tuned specialists
when_to_show: Show after explaining Flamingo's training to contextualize the claim that it outperforms fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks without fine-tuning, prompting discussion of generalization vs. specialization
suggested_caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on many tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)

description: Architecture overview of CoCa showing a unimodal image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch
teaching_value: Illustrates how CoCa unifies contrastive and generative pretraining objectives in a single architecture, helping students understand how dual objectives capture both alignment and generation capabilities
when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa to contrast its from-scratch dual-objective training with the frozen-component approaches of Flamingo and VC-GPT
suggested_caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives in one architecture. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)

description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained components are adapted to downstream tasks (image classification, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries
teaching_value: Demonstrates the concept of task-specific attention pooling as a lightweight adapter, teaching students how a single pretrained model can be efficiently repurposed for diverse vision-language tasks
when_to_show: Show after explaining CoCa's pretraining to illustrate how the same model handles recognition vs. generation tasks differently through pooler design
suggested_caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to diverse downstream tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)

description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including dual-loss computation with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.
teaching_value: Allows students to see exactly how CoCa combines contrastive and captioning losses in code form, making the abstract dual-objective training concrete and implementable.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss sum (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is applied in practice.
suggested_caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)

description: A results table comparing image captioning performance of MAGiC and other models on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised methods and standard metrics (CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).
teaching_value: Helps students understand the performance gap between unsupervised guided-decoding approaches like MAGiC and fully supervised methods, grounding the theoretical trade-offs in quantitative evidence.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC magic-score decoding formula to contextualize how well the approach performs relative to supervised baselines and other unsupervised methods.
suggested_caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating how PICa performs n-shot Visual Question Answering at inference time: an image is converted to a caption/tags, few-shot examples are selected and formatted as a prompt, and GPT-3 generates the answer.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the full inference pipeline of PICa — from image to caption to GPT-3 prompt — helping students understand how vision signals are converted to language for LLM-based VQA without fine-tuning.
when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to illustrate how image captions bridge vision and language models, especially when contrasting with end-to-end trained VQA systems.
suggested_caption: PICa n-shot VQA pipeline: image → caption → GPT-3 prompt → answer. (Yang et al., 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing PICa-Base (random in-context examples) vs. PICa-Full (similar example selection + multi-query ensembling) on the OK-VQA benchmark, showing performance gains from each improvement.
teaching_value: Quantitatively shows students how in-context example selection strategy and ensembling affect few-shot VQA performance, illustrating the importance of prompt engineering in LLM-based vision tasks.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how PICa improves over a random baseline, to motivate why similar-example retrieval and ensembling matter in few-shot LLM prompting for VQA.
suggested_caption: PICa-Full vs. PICa-Base on OK-VQA: impact of example selection and ensembling. (Yang et al., 2021)

description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework, showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation to solve multimodal tasks without additional training.
teaching_value: Provides a high-level conceptual overview of how Socratic Models compose heterogeneous pretrained models via language prompts, helping students grasp the modular, training-free multimodal reasoning paradigm.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students a visual anchor for the concept of language-mediated model composition before diving into specific task examples.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models: composing VLM, LM, and ALM via language prompts. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Step-by-step illustration of how Socratic Models generate image captions: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, fills a language prompt template, and a causal LM generates the final caption.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract Socratic Models pipeline concrete by showing exactly how VLM outputs are inserted into a structured language prompt and then used by an LM for caption generation.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the image captioning application of Socratic Models, to walk students through each step of the VLM→prompt→LM chain before presenting quantitative results.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models captioning: VLM fills prompt template, LM generates caption. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Table or chart comparing image captioning performance of Socratic Models against other models (including ClipCap and supervised baselines) on 100 random COCO test examples, using standard captioning metrics.
teaching_value: Allows students to quantitatively assess the trade-off of the training-free Socratic approach versus trained models, reinforcing the lesson that zero-shot composition has a performance cost but remains competitive.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the approach's strengths and limitations in numbers, especially when comparing training-free vs. fine-tuned methods.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models captioning vs. ClipCap on 100 COCO examples. (Zeng et al., 2022)

description: Diagram showing the Socratic Models pipeline for egocentric video understanding: audio is processed by ALM (speech-to-text), video frames by VLM (image-to-text), outputs are combined into a language prompt, and LM generates activity summaries and answers questions.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how Socratic Models scale to complex multimodal inputs (video + audio) by chaining three model types through language, helping students see the framework's generality beyond static image captioning.
when_to_show: Show when illustrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to demonstrate how audio, video, and language models can be composed for complex real-world tasks.
suggested_caption: Socratic Models for egocentric video: ALM + VLM + LM chained via language. (Zeng et al., 2022)
